linear-motion.title: Linear Motion
linear-motion.brief_info: Linear motion is the movement of a body along a straight line. In uniform linear motion, the body covers equal distances in equal time intervals.
linear-motion.example_problem:
A car moves at 72 km/h. What distance will it cover in 2.5 hours?

Solution:
v = 72 km/h
t = 2.5 h
S = v × t = 72 × 2.5 = 180 km

Answer: 180 km

uniform-accelerated.title: Uniform and Accelerated Motion
uniform-accelerated.brief_info: Uniformly accelerated motion has constant acceleration. The velocity changes uniformly over time. In uniform motion, velocity is constant and acceleration equals zero.
uniform-accelerated.example_problem:
A body starts from rest with acceleration 2 m/s². What velocity will it reach in 5 seconds?

Solution:
v₀ = 0 m/s
a = 2 m/s²
t = 5 s
v = v₀ + at = 0 + 2×5 = 10 m/s

Answer: 10 m/s

circular-motion.title: Circular Motion
circular-motion.brief_info: Circular motion is curvilinear motion along a circle. It is characterized by angular velocity, period, and frequency of revolution.
circular-motion.example_problem:
A wheel of radius 0.5 m rotates at 2 rev/s. Find the linear velocity of a point on the rim.

Solution:
R = 0.5 m
ν = 2 Hz
v = 2πRν = 2 × 3.14 × 0.5 × 2 = 6.28 m/s

Answer: 6.28 m/s

relative-motion.title: Relative Motion
relative-motion.brief_info: Motion is relative — its characteristics (velocity, trajectory) depend on the reference frame. The velocity relative to a fixed frame equals the sum of the velocity relative to the moving frame and the frame's velocity.
relative-motion.example_problem:
A passenger walks at 5 km/h relative to a train. The train moves at 60 km/h. Find the passenger's speed relative to the ground (walking in the direction of the train).

Solution:
v = v₁ + v₂ = 60 + 5 = 65 km/h

Answer: 65 km/h

motion-graphs.title: Motion Graphs
motion-graphs.brief_info: Motion graphs visually represent the dependence of position, velocity, and acceleration on time. From an x(t) graph you can determine the type of motion; from a v(t) graph you can find the distance as the area under the curve.
motion-graphs.example_problem:
From a v(t) graph, determine the distance in 4 seconds if the graph is a straight line from v=0 at t=0 to v=8 m/s at t=4 s.

Solution:
Distance = area of triangle = ½ × base × height
S = ½ × 4 × 8 = 16 m

Answer: 16 m

newton-laws.title: Newton's Laws
newton-laws.brief_info: Newton's three laws are the foundation of classical mechanics. 1st law (inertia): a body maintains its state of rest or uniform motion. 2nd law: F = ma. 3rd law: action and reaction forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
newton-laws.example_problem:
A force of 20 N acts on a body of mass 5 kg. Find the acceleration.

Solution:
m = 5 kg
F = 20 N
a = F/m = 20/5 = 4 m/s²

Answer: 4 m/s²

forces.title: Forces in Mechanics
forces.brief_info: Main forces in mechanics: gravity (F = mg), elastic force (F = kx), friction (F = μN), normal force. All forces are measured in newtons.
forces.example_problem:
Find the gravitational force acting on a body of mass 10 kg.

Solution:
m = 10 kg
g = 10 m/s²
F = mg = 10 × 10 = 100 N

Answer: 100 N

multiple-forces.title: Motion Under Multiple Forces
multiple-forces.brief_info: When several forces act on a body, its motion is determined by the resultant — the vector sum of all forces. Acceleration is found using Newton's second law.
multiple-forces.example_problem:
Two forces act on a body of mass 2 kg: F₁ = 6 N to the right and F₂ = 2 N to the left. Find the acceleration.

Solution:
F = F₁ - F₂ = 6 - 2 = 4 N
a = F/m = 4/2 = 2 m/s² (to the right)

Answer: 2 m/s²

inclined-plane.title: Inclined Plane
inclined-plane.brief_info: An inclined plane is a simple machine. Gravity decomposes into components: parallel to the plane (mgsinα) and perpendicular (mgcosα). This determines the body's motion.
inclined-plane.example_problem:
A body of mass 5 kg is on an inclined plane with angle 30°. Find the force along the plane.

Solution:
F = mg·sin(30°) = 5 × 10 × 0.5 = 25 N

Answer: 25 N

momentum.title: Momentum and Conservation of Momentum
momentum.brief_info: Momentum p = mv. Conservation of momentum: in an isolated system, total momentum remains constant. Used for analyzing collisions and explosions.
momentum.example_problem:
Two balls of masses 2 kg and 3 kg move toward each other at 4 m/s and 2 m/s. After collision they stick together. Find the velocity after collision.

Solution:
m₁v₁ - m₂v₂ = (m₁+m₂)v
2×4 - 3×2 = 5×v
v = 2/5 = 0.4 m/s

Answer: 0.4 m/s

equilibrium.title: Equilibrium Conditions
equilibrium.brief_info: A body is in equilibrium when the sum of all forces equals zero (∑F = 0) and the sum of torques about any axis equals zero (∑M = 0).
equilibrium.example_problem:
A beam of mass 20 kg rests on two supports 4 m apart. Find the reaction forces if the center of mass is in the middle.

Solution:
N₁ + N₂ = mg = 200 N
By symmetry: N₁ = N₂ = 100 N

Answer: 100 N each

torque.title: Torque
torque.brief_info: Torque M = F·d, where d is the moment arm (shortest distance from the axis of rotation to the line of action of the force). Torque causes rotation.
torque.example_problem:
A force of 40 N is applied perpendicular to a lever of length 0.5 m. Find the torque.

Solution:
M = F × d = 40 × 0.5 = 20 N·m

Answer: 20 N·m

center-mass.title: Center of Mass
center-mass.brief_info: The center of mass is the point where all mass can be considered concentrated. For uniform bodies, it coincides with the geometric center. Stable equilibrium requires the center of mass above the support area.
center-mass.example_problem:
Two balls of masses 1 kg and 3 kg are connected by a rod 40 cm long. Find the center of mass position.

Solution:
x = (m₁x₁ + m₂x₂)/(m₁+m₂)
x = (1×0 + 3×40)/(1+3) = 30 cm from the first ball

Answer: 30 cm from the first ball

simple-machines.title: Simple Machines
simple-machines.brief_info: Simple machines: lever, pulley, wheel and axle, wedge, screw. Golden rule of mechanics: gaining in force means losing in distance. Efficiency = useful work / total work.
simple-machines.example_problem:
A lever has arms of 20 cm and 60 cm. What force must be applied to the long arm to lift a 30 kg load?

Solution:
F₁l₁ = F₂l₂
F × 60 = 300 × 20
F = 100 N

Answer: 100 N

work.title: Work
work.brief_info: Mechanical work A = F·S·cosα, where α is the angle between the force and displacement. Work is measured in joules. Work of gravity: A = mgh.
work.example_problem:
Find the work done by a force of 50 N when a body moves 4 m in the direction of the force.

Solution:
A = F × S × cos(0°) = 50 × 4 × 1 = 200 J

Answer: 200 J

kinetic-energy.title: Kinetic Energy
kinetic-energy.brief_info: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion: E = mv²/2. Work-energy theorem: the work done by the net force equals the change in kinetic energy.
kinetic-energy.example_problem:
Find the kinetic energy of a car of mass 1000 kg moving at 20 m/s.

Solution:
E = mv²/2 = 1000 × 400 / 2 = 200,000 J = 200 kJ

Answer: 200 kJ

potential-energy.title: Potential Energy
potential-energy.brief_info: Potential energy is the energy of interaction. In a gravitational field: E = mgh. For a spring: E = kx²/2. Depends on the choice of the reference level.
potential-energy.example_problem:
Find the potential energy of a body of mass 5 kg at a height of 10 m.

Solution:
E = mgh = 5 × 10 × 10 = 500 J

Answer: 500 J

energy-conservation.title: Conservation of Energy
energy-conservation.brief_info: In an isolated system, total mechanical energy is conserved: E = Ek + Ep = const. In the presence of friction, mechanical energy converts to internal energy.
energy-conservation.example_problem:
A body falls from a height of 20 m. Find the velocity at the ground (neglecting air resistance).

Solution:
mgh = mv²/2
v = √(2gh) = √(2×10×20) = 20 m/s

Answer: 20 m/s

harmonic-oscillations.title: Harmonic Oscillations
harmonic-oscillations.brief_info: Harmonic oscillations are described by x = A·cos(ωt + φ), where A is amplitude, ω is angular frequency, φ is initial phase. Period T = 2π/ω.
harmonic-oscillations.example_problem:
A body oscillates with amplitude 5 cm and period 2 s. Find the maximum velocity.

Solution:
v_max = ωA = (2π/T)A = (2π/2) × 0.05 = 0.157 m/s

Answer: 15.7 cm/s

pendulums.title: Pendulums
pendulums.brief_info: Simple pendulum: T = 2π√(l/g). Spring pendulum: T = 2π√(m/k). The period does not depend on amplitude (for small angles).
pendulums.example_problem:
Find the period of a simple pendulum of length 1 m.

Solution:
T = 2π√(l/g) = 2π√(1/10) = 2π × 0.316 ≈ 2 s

Answer: ≈ 2 s

mechanical-waves.title: Mechanical Waves
mechanical-waves.brief_info: A mechanical wave is the propagation of oscillations in a medium. Characteristics: wavelength λ, speed v, frequency ν. Relation: v = λν. Waves can be longitudinal or transverse.
mechanical-waves.example_problem:
The speed of sound in air is 340 m/s. Find the wavelength at a frequency of 680 Hz.

Solution:
λ = v/ν = 340/680 = 0.5 m

Answer: 0.5 m

resonance.title: Resonance
resonance.brief_info: Resonance is a sharp increase in amplitude of forced oscillations when the driving frequency matches the natural frequency. Used in music, radio engineering.
resonance.example_problem:
The natural frequency of a bridge is 2 Hz. At what marching frequency will resonance occur?

Solution:
Resonance occurs when ν_ext = ν_natural = 2 Hz

Answer: 2 Hz (2 steps per second)

matter-structure.title: Structure of Matter
matter-structure.brief_info: All substances consist of molecules (atoms) in continuous random motion. Attractive and repulsive forces act between molecules.
matter-structure.example_problem:
How many molecules are in 36 g of water?

Solution:
M(H₂O) = 18 g/mol
ν = m/M = 36/18 = 2 mol
N = νNₐ = 2 × 6.02×10²³ = 1.2×10²⁴

Answer: 1.2×10²⁴ molecules

temperature.title: Temperature
temperature.brief_info: Temperature is a measure of average kinetic energy of molecules. Relation: E = (3/2)kT. Absolute zero: T = 0 K = -273°C.
temperature.example_problem:
Find the average kinetic energy of a gas molecule at 27°C.

Solution:
T = 273 + 27 = 300 K
E = (3/2)kT = 1.5 × 1.38×10⁻²³ × 300 = 6.21×10⁻²¹ J

Answer: 6.21×10⁻²¹ J

gas-pressure.title: Gas Pressure
gas-pressure.brief_info: Gas pressure is created by molecular impacts on container walls. Fundamental MKT equation: p = (1/3)nm₀v². Pressure depends on concentration and temperature.
gas-pressure.example_problem:
Find gas pressure if molecular concentration is 10²⁵ m⁻³, RMS speed is 500 m/s, molecular mass is 5×10⁻²⁶ kg.

Solution:
p = (1/3)nm₀v² = (1/3)×10²⁵×5×10⁻²⁶×250000 ≈ 42 kPa

Answer: ≈ 42 kPa

thermal-conductivity.title: Thermal Conductivity
thermal-conductivity.brief_info: Thermal conductivity is heat transfer through particle interaction without mass transfer. Best conductors — metals, worst — gases and porous materials.

convection.title: Convection
convection.brief_info: Convection is heat transfer by flows of liquid or gas. Heated layers rise, cold ones descend. Used in heating and ventilation.

radiation.title: Radiation
radiation.brief_info: Radiation is energy transfer by electromagnetic waves. No medium required. Dark bodies absorb and emit better than light ones. Stefan-Boltzmann law: P = σT⁴.

heat-quantity.title: Quantity of Heat
heat-quantity.brief_info: Heat is energy transferred during heat exchange. Q = cmΔT for heating, Q = λm for melting, Q = Lm for vaporization.
heat-quantity.example_problem:
How much heat is needed to heat 2 kg of water from 20°C to 80°C?

Solution:
Q = cmΔT = 4200 × 2 × 60 = 504,000 J = 504 kJ

Answer: 504 kJ

state-equation.title: Equation of State
state-equation.brief_info: Ideal gas law (Mendeleev-Clapeyron): pV = νRT relates pressure, volume, and temperature. R = 8.31 J/(mol·K) — universal gas constant.
state-equation.example_problem:
Find the volume of 2 mol of gas at 100 kPa and 300 K.

Solution:
V = νRT/p = 2×8.31×300/100000 = 0.05 m³

Answer: 50 L

isoprocesses.title: Isoprocesses
isoprocesses.brief_info: Isoprocesses maintain one parameter constant. Isotherm (T=const): pV=const. Isobar (p=const): V/T=const. Isochor (V=const): p/T=const.
isoprocesses.example_problem:
Gas at 300 K occupies 10 L. What volume will it occupy at 600 K (isobaric)?

Solution:
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
V₂ = V₁T₂/T₁ = 10×600/300 = 20 L

Answer: 20 L

gas-laws.title: Gas Laws
gas-laws.brief_info: Boyle's law (isotherm), Gay-Lussac's law (isobar), Charles's law (isochor). Combined gas law: pV/T = const.
gas-laws.example_problem:
Gas is compressed isothermally from 5 L to 2 L. Initial pressure is 100 kPa. Find the final pressure.

Solution:
p₁V₁ = p₂V₂
p₂ = p₁V₁/V₂ = 100×5/2 = 250 kPa

Answer: 250 kPa

first-law.title: First Law of Thermodynamics
first-law.brief_info: First law: Q = ΔU + A — heat goes into changing internal energy and doing work. This is conservation of energy for thermal processes.
first-law.example_problem:
500 J of heat is transferred to a gas, and it does 200 J of work. How did the internal energy change?

Solution:
ΔU = Q - A = 500 - 200 = 300 J

Answer: increased by 300 J

second-law.title: Second Law of Thermodynamics
second-law.brief_info: The second law determines the direction of thermal processes. Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot one. Entropy of an isolated system never decreases.

heat-engines.title: Heat Engines
heat-engines.brief_info: A heat engine converts heat into work. It consists of a heater, working substance, and cooler. Examples: ICE, steam turbine, refrigerator.

efficiency.title: Efficiency
efficiency.brief_info: Efficiency of a heat engine: η = A/Q₁ = (Q₁-Q₂)/Q₁. Maximum efficiency is the Carnot cycle: η = (T₁-T₂)/T₁. Efficiency is always less than 100%.
efficiency.example_problem:
Heater temperature 500 K, cooler 300 K. Find the maximum efficiency.

Solution:
η = (T₁-T₂)/T₁ = (500-300)/500 = 0.4 = 40%

Answer: 40%

melting.title: Melting
melting.brief_info: Melting is the transition from solid to liquid at constant temperature. Requires heat of fusion Q = λm. The reverse process is crystallization.
melting.example_problem:
How much heat is needed to melt 2 kg of ice at 0°C? (λ = 330 kJ/kg)

Solution:
Q = λm = 330,000 × 2 = 660,000 J = 660 kJ

Answer: 660 kJ

evaporation.title: Evaporation
evaporation.brief_info: Evaporation is the transition from liquid to vapor from the surface at any temperature. Requires heat of vaporization. Rate depends on temperature, surface area, humidity.

boiling.title: Boiling
boiling.brief_info: Boiling is intense vaporization throughout the liquid at a specific temperature. Boiling point depends on pressure. Q = Lm.
boiling.example_problem:
How much heat is needed to vaporize 0.5 kg of water at 100°C? (L = 2.3 MJ/kg)

Solution:
Q = Lm = 2,300,000 × 0.5 = 1,150,000 J = 1.15 MJ

Answer: 1.15 MJ

phase-diagrams.title: Phase Diagrams
phase-diagrams.brief_info: A phase diagram shows regions of substance phases in p-T coordinates. Triple point — coexistence of all three phases. Critical point — disappearance of the liquid-gas boundary.

electric-charge.title: Electric Charge
electric-charge.brief_info: Electric charge is a property of bodies to interact via electromagnetic fields. Charge is quantized: q = ne (e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C). Charge is conserved.
electric-charge.example_problem:
How many electrons must be removed for a body to acquire a charge of 3.2×10⁻¹⁸ C?

Solution:
n = q/e = 3.2×10⁻¹⁸ / 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ = 20 electrons

Answer: 20 electrons

coulomb-law.title: Coulomb's Law
coulomb-law.brief_info: Force between two point charges: F = k|q₁q₂|/r², where k = 9×10⁹ N·m²/C². Like charges repel, unlike charges attract.
coulomb-law.example_problem:
Find the force between two charges of 1 μC at a distance of 3 m.

Solution:
F = kq₁q₂/r² = 9×10⁹ × 10⁻⁶ × 10⁻⁶ / 9 = 0.001 N = 1 mN

Answer: 1 mN

electric-field.title: Electric Field
electric-field.brief_info: An electric field is a form of matter through which charges interact. Characterized by field strength E = F/q. Field lines go from + to -.
electric-field.example_problem:
Find the field strength of a 2 μC point charge at 1 m distance.

Solution:
E = kq/r² = 9×10⁹ × 2×10⁻⁶ / 1 = 18,000 V/m = 18 kV/m

Answer: 18 kV/m

potential-voltage.title: Potential and Voltage
potential-voltage.brief_info: Potential φ = W/q — energy characteristic of the field. Voltage U = φ₁ - φ₂ = A/q. Relation with field strength: E = U/d (uniform field).
potential-voltage.example_problem:
Find the work done moving a charge of 5 μC between points with a potential difference of 100 V.

Solution:
A = qU = 5×10⁻⁶ × 100 = 5×10⁻⁴ J = 0.5 mJ

Answer: 0.5 mJ

capacitors.title: Capacitors
capacitors.brief_info: A capacitor stores electric charge. Capacitance C = q/U. For a parallel plate capacitor: C = ε₀εS/d. Energy: W = CU²/2 = q²/2C.
capacitors.example_problem:
Find the capacitance if, at a voltage of 100 V, it accumulates a charge of 5 μC.

Solution:
C = q/U = 5×10⁻⁶ / 100 = 5×10⁻⁸ F = 50 nF

Answer: 50 nF

current-strength.title: Electric Current
current-strength.brief_info: Current I = q/t — charge passing through a cross-section per unit time. Measured in amperes. Current direction is the direction of positive charge movement.
current-strength.example_problem:
A charge of 50 C passes through a conductor in 10 s. Find the current.

Solution:
I = q/t = 50/10 = 5 A

Answer: 5 A

ohm-law.title: Ohm's Law
ohm-law.brief_info: Ohm's law for a circuit section: I = U/R. For a complete circuit: I = ε/(R+r), where ε is EMF, r is internal resistance.
ohm-law.example_problem:
Find the current at voltage 12 V and resistance 4 Ω.

Solution:
I = U/R = 12/4 = 3 A

Answer: 3 A

work-power.title: Work and Power of Current
work-power.brief_info: Work of current A = UIt = I²Rt = U²t/R. Power P = UI = I²R = U²/R. Joule-Lenz law: Q = I²Rt — heat produced by a conductor.
work-power.example_problem:
Find the power consumed by a lamp at 220 V and current 0.5 A.

Solution:
P = UI = 220 × 0.5 = 110 W

Answer: 110 W

conductor-connections.title: Conductor Connections
conductor-connections.brief_info: Series: R = R₁+R₂, I is the same, U = U₁+U₂. Parallel: 1/R = 1/R₁+1/R₂, U is the same, I = I₁+I₂.
conductor-connections.example_problem:
Find the total resistance of two 6 Ω resistors connected in parallel.

Solution:
1/R = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3
R = 3 Ω

Answer: 3 Ω

magnetic-field.title: Magnetic Field
magnetic-field.brief_info: A magnetic field is created by moving charges and acts on moving charges. Characterized by induction B (T). Field lines are closed.

ampere-force.title: Ampere's Force
ampere-force.brief_info: Ampere's force acts on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field: F = BILsinα. Direction is determined by the left-hand rule.
ampere-force.example_problem:
Find the force on a 0.5 m conductor carrying 2 A in a 0.1 T field (perpendicular).

Solution:
F = BIL = 0.1 × 2 × 0.5 = 0.1 N

Answer: 0.1 N

lorentz-force.title: Lorentz Force
lorentz-force.brief_info: The Lorentz force acts on a moving charge in a magnetic field: F = qvBsinα. It causes the charge to move in a circle or spiral.
lorentz-force.example_problem:
Find the radius of an electron's path at 10⁶ m/s in a 0.01 T magnetic field.

Solution:
R = mv/(qB) = 9.1×10⁻³¹ × 10⁶ / (1.6×10⁻¹⁹ × 0.01) ≈ 0.57 mm

Answer: ≈ 0.57 mm

faraday-law.title: Faraday's Law
faraday-law.brief_info: Induced EMF equals the rate of change of magnetic flux: ε = -dΦ/dt. The minus sign is Lenz's rule. This is the basis of generators.
faraday-law.example_problem:
Magnetic flux through a coil changed by 0.5 Wb in 0.1 s. Find the induced EMF.

Solution:
ε = ΔΦ/Δt = 0.5/0.1 = 5 V

Answer: 5 V

lenz-rule.title: Lenz's Rule
lenz-rule.brief_info: The induced current flows in a direction to oppose the cause that produced it. This is a consequence of the conservation of energy.

inductance.title: Inductance
inductance.brief_info: Inductance L characterizes a coil's ability to create magnetic flux: Φ = LI. Self-induced EMF: ε = -L(dI/dt). Magnetic field energy: W = LI²/2.
inductance.example_problem:
Find the energy of the magnetic field of a coil with inductance 0.5 H at current 2 A.

Solution:
W = LI²/2 = 0.5 × 4 / 2 = 1 J

Answer: 1 J

eddy-currents.title: Eddy Currents
eddy-currents.brief_info: Eddy currents (Foucault currents) are induced currents in bulk conductors. They cause heating. Used in induction furnaces and dampers.

sinusoidal-current.title: Sinusoidal Current
sinusoidal-current.brief_info: Alternating current follows a sinusoidal law: i = I₀sin(ωt). Characterized by amplitude, frequency, and phase. RMS value: I = I₀/√2.
sinusoidal-current.example_problem:
The peak alternating current is 10 A. Find the RMS value.

Solution:
I = I₀/√2 = 10/1.41 ≈ 7.07 A

Answer: ≈ 7.07 A

reactive-resistance.title: Reactive Resistance
reactive-resistance.brief_info: Inductive reactance: X_L = ωL. Capacitive: X_C = 1/(ωC). Impedance: Z = √(R² + (X_L - X_C)²).
reactive-resistance.example_problem:
Find the inductive reactance of a 0.1 H coil at 50 Hz.

Solution:
X_L = 2πfL = 2π × 50 × 0.1 ≈ 31.4 Ω

Answer: ≈ 31.4 Ω

transformers.title: Transformers
transformers.brief_info: A transformer changes AC voltage. Transformation ratio: k = U₁/U₂ = n₁/n₂. k>1 — step-down, k<1 — step-up.
transformers.example_problem:
Primary winding has 1000 turns, secondary 50 turns. Find the output voltage with 220 V input.

Solution:
U₂ = U₁n₂/n₁ = 220 × 50/1000 = 11 V

Answer: 11 V

power-transmission.title: Power Transmission
power-transmission.brief_info: To reduce losses (P = I²R), electricity is transmitted at high voltage. At the power plant voltage is stepped up, at the consumer it is stepped down using transformers.

light-propagation.title: Propagation of Light
light-propagation.brief_info: Light travels in straight lines in a uniform medium. Speed of light in vacuum c = 3×10⁸ m/s. In a medium, speed is lower: v = c/n, where n is the refractive index.
light-propagation.example_problem:
Find the speed of light in water (n = 1.33).

Solution:
v = c/n = 3×10⁸/1.33 ≈ 2.26×10⁸ m/s

Answer: ≈ 2.26×10⁸ m/s

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.brief_info: Law of reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection (α = β). Rays lie in the same plane as the normal. Specular reflection is from smooth surfaces.
reflection.example_problem:
A ray hits a mirror at 30° to the surface. Find the angle between the incident and reflected rays.

Solution:
Angle of incidence to normal: 90° - 30° = 60°
Angle between rays: 60° + 60° = 120°

Answer: 120°

refraction.title: Refraction
refraction.brief_info: Snell's law: n₁sinα = n₂sinβ. When entering a denser medium, light bends toward the normal. Total internal reflection: sinα_cr = n₂/n₁.
refraction.example_problem:
A ray passes from air to water (n=1.33) at 45°. Find the angle of refraction.

Solution:
sinβ = sinα/n = sin45°/1.33 = 0.707/1.33 ≈ 0.53
β ≈ 32°

Answer: ≈ 32°

lenses-mirrors.title: Lenses and Mirrors
lenses-mirrors.brief_info: Thin lens formula: 1/F = 1/d + 1/f. Optical power D = 1/F (diopters). Magnification: Γ = f/d = H/h. Converging lens: F>0, diverging: F<0.
lenses-mirrors.example_problem:
Focal length of a lens is 20 cm. Object is at 30 cm. Find the image distance.

Solution:
1/f = 1/F - 1/d = 1/20 - 1/30 = 1/60
f = 60 cm

Answer: 60 cm

optical-devices.title: Optical Instruments
optical-devices.brief_info: Eye, magnifying glass, microscope, telescope, camera — optical instruments. Magnifying glass: Γ = 25/F cm. Microscope: Γ = Γ_obj × Γ_eye. Telescope: Γ = F_obj/F_eye.
optical-devices.example_problem:
Find the magnification of a magnifying glass with focal length 5 cm.

Solution:
Γ = 25/F = 25/5 = 5×

Answer: 5× magnification

interference.title: Interference
interference.brief_info: Interference is the superposition of coherent waves. Maximum at path difference Δ = kλ, minimum at Δ = (2k+1)λ/2. Used in interferometers and lens coating.

diffraction.title: Diffraction
diffraction.brief_info: Diffraction is the bending of waves around obstacles. Diffraction grating: d·sinφ = kλ. Used in spectral analysis.
diffraction.example_problem:
Grating period is 0.01 mm. Find the diffraction angle for λ = 500 nm (1st order).

Solution:
sinφ = λ/d = 500×10⁻⁹/10⁻⁵ = 0.05
φ ≈ 2.9°

Answer: ≈ 2.9°

polarization.title: Polarization
polarization.brief_info: Polarization is selecting oscillations in one plane. Light is a transverse wave. Malus's law: I = I₀cos²φ. Used in 3D glasses and LCD displays.

photoelectric-effect.title: Photoelectric Effect
photoelectric-effect.brief_info: Photoelectric effect is the ejection of electrons by light. Einstein's equation: hν = A + mv²/2. Red limit: ν₀ = A/h. Photoelectric laws confirmed the quantum nature of light.
photoelectric-effect.example_problem:
Work function is 2 eV. Find the red limit of the photoelectric effect.

Solution:
ν₀ = A/h = 2×1.6×10⁻¹⁹/(6.63×10⁻³⁴) ≈ 4.8×10¹⁴ Hz

Answer: ≈ 4.8×10¹⁴ Hz

light-dualism.title: Wave-Particle Duality of Light
light-dualism.brief_info: Light exhibits both wave (interference, diffraction) and particle (photoelectric effect) properties. A photon has energy E = hν and momentum p = h/λ.
light-dualism.example_problem:
Find the momentum of a photon with wavelength 500 nm.

Solution:
p = h/λ = 6.63×10⁻³⁴/500×10⁻⁹ = 1.33×10⁻²⁷ kg·m/s

Answer: 1.33×10⁻²⁷ kg·m/s

emission-spectra.title: Emission Spectra
emission-spectra.brief_info: Spectra: continuous (heated solids), line (atoms), band (molecules). Spectral analysis determines the composition of a substance.

lasers.title: Lasers
lasers.brief_info: A laser is a source of coherent monochromatic radiation. Based on stimulated emission. Used in medicine, communications, and technology.

atom-models.title: Atomic Models
atom-models.brief_info: Thomson model (plum pudding), Rutherford's planetary model, Bohr model with quantum orbits. Modern quantum mechanical model describes electron clouds.

energy-levels.title: Energy Levels
energy-levels.brief_info: Electron energy in atoms is quantized. For hydrogen: E_n = -13.6/n² eV. When transitioning between levels, a photon with energy hν = E₂ - E₁ is emitted or absorbed.
energy-levels.example_problem:
Find the photon energy for an electron transition from the 3rd to 2nd level in hydrogen.

Solution:
E₃ = -13.6/9 = -1.51 eV
E₂ = -13.6/4 = -3.4 eV
hν = E₃ - E₂ = 1.89 eV

Answer: 1.89 eV

electron-shells.title: Electron Shells
electron-shells.brief_info: Electrons reside in shells (K, L, M...). Maximum number of electrons per shell: 2n². The Pauli exclusion principle forbids identical quantum states.

de-broglie-waves.title: De Broglie Waves
de-broglie-waves.brief_info: Every particle has wave properties. De Broglie wavelength: λ = h/p = h/(mv). Wave properties manifest in microparticles.
de-broglie-waves.example_problem:
Find the wavelength of an electron with energy 100 eV.

Solution:
λ = h/√(2mE) = 6.63×10⁻³⁴/√(2×9.1×10⁻³¹×1.6×10⁻¹⁷)
λ ≈ 0.12 nm

Answer: ≈ 0.12 nm

heisenberg-uncertainty.title: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
heisenberg-uncertainty.brief_info: It is impossible to simultaneously measure the coordinate and momentum of a particle exactly: Δx·Δp ≥ ℏ/2. Similarly for energy and time: ΔE·Δt ≥ ℏ/2.

nucleus-structure.title: Nuclear Structure
nucleus-structure.brief_info: The nucleus consists of protons and neutrons (nucleons). Atomic number Z — number of protons. Mass number A = Z + N. Isotopes have the same Z but different N.
nucleus-structure.example_problem:
How many protons and neutrons are in the ²³⁸U nucleus?

Solution:
Z = 92 (protons)
N = A - Z = 238 - 92 = 146 (neutrons)

Answer: 92 protons, 146 neutrons

nuclear-forces.title: Nuclear Forces
nuclear-forces.brief_info: Nuclear forces are attractive forces between nucleons. Short-range (≈10⁻¹⁵ m), charge-independent, exhibit saturation. Much stronger than electromagnetic forces.

binding-energy.title: Binding Energy
binding-energy.brief_info: Binding energy is the energy needed to separate a nucleus into nucleons. E_b = Δm·c². Mass defect: Δm = Zm_p + Nm_n - M_nucleus. Specific binding energy is maximum for Fe.

decay-types.title: Types of Decay
decay-types.brief_info: α-decay: ⁴He is emitted (Z→Z-2, A→A-4). β-decay: electron emitted (Z→Z+1, A=const). γ-radiation: electromagnetic radiation (Z, A unchanged).
decay-types.example_problem:
Write the α-decay reaction of ²²⁶Ra.

Solution:
²²⁶Ra → ²²²Rn + ⁴He
(88→86, 226→222)

Answer: ²²⁶Ra → ²²²Rn + ⁴He

decay-law.title: Radioactive Decay Law
decay-law.brief_info: N = N₀·2^(-t/T), where T is the half-life. During time T, half the nuclei decay. Activity A = λN decreases by the same law.
decay-law.example_problem:
Half-life of iodine-131 is 8 days. How much remains after 24 days?

Solution:
t/T = 24/8 = 3
N = N₀/2³ = N₀/8 = 12.5%

Answer: 12.5% of the initial amount

radiation-doses.title: Radiation Doses
radiation-doses.brief_info: Absorbed dose D (Gy) is energy per unit mass. Equivalent dose H (Sv) = D·k accounts for radiation type. Population limit ~1 mSv/year.

fission.title: Nuclear Fission
fission.brief_info: Fission of heavy nuclei (U, Pu) upon neutron capture. Releases ~200 MeV and 2-3 neutrons → chain reaction. Basis of nuclear power plants and atomic bombs.

fusion.title: Thermonuclear Fusion
fusion.brief_info: Fusion of light nuclei (H, He) at high temperatures. Energy source of the Sun and stars. Condition: T > 10⁷ K. Promising energy source (tokamak).

nuclear-energy-use.title: Applications of Nuclear Energy
nuclear-energy-use.brief_info: Nuclear power plants use controlled chain reactions. Advantages: large energy from small fuel amounts. Challenges: radioactive waste, safety concerns.

universal-gravitation.title: Law of Universal Gravitation
universal-gravitation.brief_info: Any two bodies attract with F = G·m₁·m₂/r², G ≈ 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg². The force lies along the line joining their centers.
universal-gravitation.example_problem:
Two 100 kg spheres touch (center distance r = 0.2 m). Estimate the gravitational force.

F = Gm²/r² ≈ 1.7×10⁻⁵ N — tiny for everyday masses.

gravitational-field.title: Gravitational Field and Field Strength
gravitational-field.brief_info: The field is described by strength γ = F/m. For a point mass M: γ = GM/r², directed toward the source.
gravitational-field.example_problem: Near Earth's surface, γ ≈ GM/R² ≈ 9.8 m/s² — the same as g.

weight-weightlessness.title: Weight and Weightlessness
weight-weightlessness.brief_info: Weight is the force on a support or suspension. In an upward-accelerating elevator, weight exceeds mg; in free fall or orbit, apparent weight can be zero (weightlessness).
weight-weightlessness.example_problem: Elevator accelerates upward at 2 m/s². Weight of a 70 kg person? P = m(g+a) = 840 N.

satellites-orbits.title: Artificial Satellites and Orbits
satellites-orbits.brief_info: For a circular orbit, gravity provides centripetal force: v = √(GM/r), period T = 2πr/v.
satellites-orbits.example_problem: Higher orbit means lower speed and longer period (Kepler).

kepler-laws.title: Kepler's Laws
kepler-laws.brief_info: 1) Orbits are ellipses with the Sun at a focus. 2) Equal areas in equal times. 3) T²/a³ is the same for all planets.
kepler-laws.example_problem: If Mars's period is 1.88 Earth years, its semimajor axis is about 1.52 AU.

cosmic-velocities.title: First, Second, and Third Cosmic Velocities
cosmic-velocities.brief_info: I: circular orbit near Earth ≈ 7.9 km/s. II: escape from Earth ≈ 11.2 km/s. III: escape from the Solar System from Earth ≈ 16.7 km/s.
cosmic-velocities.example_problem: v₂/v₁ = √2 because escape requires zero total mechanical energy vs negative for a bound circular orbit.

gravitational-energy.title: Gravitational Potential Energy
gravitational-energy.brief_info: For two point masses, E_p = -Gm₁m₂/r (zero at infinity). Gravitational work is path-independent.
gravitational-energy.example_problem: Raising a satellite from R to 2R increases (makes less negative) potential energy by GMm/(2R).

liquid-pressure.title: Pressure in Liquids
liquid-pressure.brief_info: Hydrostatic pressure p = p₀ + ρgh increases with depth; at rest, pressure is the same on the same horizontal level.
liquid-pressure.example_problem: Extra pressure 2×10⁵ Pa in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³): depth h = p/(ρg) = 20 m.

pascal-law.title: Pascal's Law
pascal-law.brief_info: Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished — basis of hydraulic presses and brakes.
pascal-law.example_problem: Same pressure on small and large pistons: F₂ = p·S₂ can be much larger than F₁.

archimedes-principle.title: Archimedes' Principle
archimedes-principle.brief_info: Buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid: F_A = ρ_fluid·g·V_submerged.
archimedes-principle.example_problem: Fully submerged 0.5 dm³ block in water: F_A ≈ 5 N.

floating-bodies.title: Flotation and Submersion
floating-bodies.brief_info: A body floats if average density is less than the fluid or if buoyancy balances weight. Fraction submerged: V_sub/V = ρ_body/ρ_fluid.
floating-bodies.example_problem: Wood with ρ = 600 kg/m³ floating on water: 60% submerged, 40% above the surface.

atmospheric-pressure.title: Atmospheric Pressure
atmospheric-pressure.brief_info: Air column at sea level gives p_atm ≈ 10⁵ Pa ≈ 760 mm Hg; pressure drops with altitude.
atmospheric-pressure.example_problem: Force on 150 cm² palm at 10⁵ Pa: F = pS ≈ 1500 N.

barometer-manometer.title: Barometer and Manometer
barometer-manometer.brief_info: Mercury barometer height h = p/(ρg). Manometers measure gauge pressure relative to atmosphere.
barometer-manometer.example_problem: Water barometer for 10⁵ Pa needs h ≈ 10 m — impractical; mercury is used instead.

hydraulic-machines.title: Hydraulic Machines
hydraulic-machines.brief_info: Hydraulic presses multiply force via Pascal's law; energy is conserved when piston displacements are accounted for.
hydraulic-machines.example_problem: Efficiency links input work to useful output work on the load.

continuity-equation.title: Continuity Equation
continuity-equation.brief_info: Steady flow: S₁v₁ = S₂v₂ — narrower cross section means higher speed.

bernoulli-equation.title: Bernoulli's Equation
bernoulli-equation.brief_info: Along a streamline for an ideal fluid: p + ρv²/2 + ρgh = const. Faster flow tends to mean lower pressure.
bernoulli-equation.example_problem: Used qualitatively for lift, atomizers, and Venturi meters.

fluid-viscosity.title: Fluid Viscosity
fluid-viscosity.brief_info: Viscosity η describes internal friction between fluid layers; important for pipe flow and Stokes drag.
fluid-viscosity.example_problem: Honey flows more slowly than water mainly because η is much larger.

stokes-law.title: Stokes' Law (Drag Force)
stokes-law.brief_info: For a small sphere in laminar flow: F = 6πηRv — sets terminal speed for microscopic drops and sedimentation.
stokes-law.example_problem: Terminal velocity balances weight, buoyancy, and viscous drag.

sound-waves.title: Sound Waves
sound-waves.brief_info: Longitudinal mechanical waves; need a medium. v = λν relates speed, wavelength, and frequency.
sound-waves.example_problem: 440 Hz in air at 340 m/s: λ ≈ 0.77 m.

sound-speed-media.title: Speed of Sound in Different Media
sound-speed-media.brief_info: Generally fastest in solids, then liquids, then gases; in air near 20°C v ≈ 343 m/s, in water ~1500 m/s.
sound-speed-media.example_problem: Sound arrives faster underwater than through air over the same path length.

loudness-intensity.title: Loudness and Intensity
loudness-intensity.brief_info: Intensity I is power per area (W/m²). Perceived loudness grows roughly logarithmically with I; point sources give I ∝ 1/r².
loudness-intensity.example_problem: Doubling distance from a point source reduces I by a factor of four.

decibel-scale.title: Decibel Scale
decibel-scale.brief_info: Sound level L = 10·log₁₀(I/I₀) dB with I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m². Each +10 dB corresponds to a 10× intensity increase.
decibel-scale.example_problem: I increases 100× → level rises by 20 dB.

doppler-sound.title: Doppler Effect for Sound
doppler-sound.brief_info: Motion of source or observer shifts observed frequency — higher when approaching, lower when receding.
doppler-sound.example_problem: Ambulance siren pitch drops as it passes you.

acoustic-resonance.title: Resonance in Acoustics
acoustic-resonance.brief_info: Air columns and strings have natural frequencies; driving at those frequencies produces large amplitude (organ pipes, guitars).
acoustic-resonance.example_problem: Open pipe length selects harmonics of standing sound waves.

ultrasound-infrasound.title: Ultrasound and Infrasound
ultrasound-infrasound.brief_info: Ultrasound (>20 kHz): imaging, sonar. Infrasound (<20 Hz): earthquakes, explosions; felt more than heard.

timbre-instruments.title: Timbre and Musical Instruments
timbre-instruments.brief_info: Timbre comes from the mix of harmonics and temporal envelope — same note, different instruments.
timbre-instruments.example_problem: Spectrum distinguishes violin from flute at the same fundamental frequency.

sound-reflection-absorption.title: Reflection and Absorption of Sound
sound-reflection-absorption.brief_info: Hard walls reflect; porous materials absorb. Reverberation is multiple reflections decaying over time.
sound-reflection-absorption.example_problem: Concert halls are designed to balance clarity and reverberation time.

einstein-postulates.title: Einstein's Postulates
einstein-postulates.brief_info: (1) Physics laws are the same in all inertial frames. (2) c in vacuum is the same for all observers, regardless of source motion.
einstein-postulates.example_problem: These require Lorentz transformations instead of Galilean velocity addition at high speeds.

lorentz-transformations.title: Lorentz Transformations
lorentz-transformations.brief_info: Relate coordinates and time between inertial frames moving at relative speed v; γ = 1/√(1−v²/c²).

time-dilation.title: Time Dilation
time-dilation.brief_info: Moving clocks tick slower from the lab frame: Δt = γ·Δt₀ for proper time Δt₀.
time-dilation.example_problem: Fast muons reach the ground because their lifetime is dilated in Earth's frame.

length-contraction.title: Length Contraction
length-contraction.brief_info: Lengths along motion shrink in the lab frame: L = L₀/γ; transverse sizes unchanged.
length-contraction.example_problem: γ = 2 halves the measured length of a rod moving along itself.

invariant-interval.title: Invariant Spacetime Interval
invariant-interval.brief_info: s² = (cΔt)² − Δr² is the same in all inertial frames; light rays have s = 0.
invariant-interval.example_problem: Interval invariance underpins causality and light cones.

mass-energy-emc2.title: Mass–Energy Relation E = mc²
mass-energy-emc2.brief_info: Rest energy E₀ = mc²; mass and energy are equivalent; nuclear reactions convert small mass defects to large energy.
mass-energy-emc2.example_problem: 9×10¹³ J corresponds to about 1 g mass difference via ΔE = Δm·c².

relativistic-momentum.title: Relativistic Momentum
relativistic-momentum.brief_info: p = γmv replaces mv; momentum grows without bound as v → c, consistent with limited v.

relativistic-energy.title: Relativistic Energy
relativistic-energy.brief_info: Total energy E = γmc²; kinetic K = (γ−1)mc²; invariant relation E² = (pc)² + (mc²)².
relativistic-energy.example_problem: When γ = 2, kinetic energy equals rest energy.

lightspeed-limit.title: Why the Speed of Light Cannot Be Reached
lightspeed-limit.brief_info: Accelerating a massive particle toward c requires unbounded energy as γ → ∞; massless quanta move exactly at c.
lightspeed-limit.example_problem: No finite work can push m₀ > 0 to exactly c.

solar-system-structure.title: The Solar System
solar-system-structure.brief_info: Sun, eight planets, dwarf planets, asteroids, comets, rings; inner rocky planets vs outer gas and ice giants.
solar-system-structure.example_problem: Composition gradient reflects temperature during planet formation.

orbits-satellites.title: Planetary and Satellite Orbits
orbits-satellites.brief_info: Nearly Keplerian ellipses; moons obey the same gravity. Geostationary orbit: 24 h period above the equator.
orbits-satellites.example_problem: The Moon shows one face due to tidal locking.

tides.title: Tides
tides.brief_info: Differential gravity from Moon and Sun deforms oceans; spring tides at new and full Moon.
tides.example_problem: Sun and Moon aligned → stronger tidal bulge.

eclipses.title: Solar and Lunar Eclipses
eclipses.brief_info: Solar: Moon blocks the Sun (new Moon). Lunar: Earth blocks sunlight reaching the Moon (full Moon).
eclipses.example_problem: Not every new Moon eclipses the Sun because the Moon's orbit is inclined to the ecliptic.

sun-structure-activity.title: The Sun: Structure and Activity
sun-structure-activity.brief_info: Core fusion, radiative and convective zones, photosphere, chromosphere, corona; sunspots and flares are magnetic activity.
sun-structure-activity.example_problem: Solar energy comes from hydrogen fusion in the core.

stellar-spectra-hr.title: Stellar Spectra and HR Diagram
stellar-spectra-hr.brief_info: Spectral type reveals surface temperature; HR diagram plots luminosity vs temperature — main sequence, giants, white dwarfs.
stellar-spectra-hr.example_problem: Red giants lie above/right of the main sequence.

stellar-evolution-types.title: Stellar Evolution and Types
stellar-evolution-types.brief_info: From cloud collapse to main sequence, then red giant, planetary nebula and white dwarf for Sun-like stars; massive stars may explode as supernovae.
stellar-evolution-types.example_problem: The Sun's end state: white dwarf + expelled shell.

compact-stars.title: Neutron Stars, Pulsars, and Black Holes
compact-stars.brief_info: Core collapse can yield neutron stars (beamed emission → pulsars) or black holes beyond the Schwarzschild limit.
compact-stars.example_problem: Pulsar pulses are like a lighthouse beam from a spinning neutron star.

milky-way-galaxies.title: The Milky Way and Galaxy Types
milky-way-galaxies.brief_info: Our barred spiral with disk and halo; types include spirals, ellipticals, irregulars; central supermassive black hole.
milky-way-galaxies.example_problem: Order-of-magnitude ~10¹¹ stars in the Milky Way.

hubble-expansion.title: Expanding Universe and Hubble's Law
hubble-expansion.brief_info: Galaxies recede on average: v ≈ H₀·d; redshift measures recession for distant objects.
hubble-expansion.example_problem: H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc gives distance from recession velocity.

big-bang-cmb.title: Big Bang and Cosmic Microwave Background
big-bang-cmb.brief_info: Hot early universe cooled; CMB at ~2.7 K is relic radiation with tiny anisotropies.
big-bang-cmb.example_problem: CMB supports a hot, dense early state and subsequent expansion.

dark-cosmos-gr.title: Dark Matter, Dark Energy, and GR (brief)
dark-cosmos-gr.brief_info: Dark matter explains flat rotation curves and lensing; dark energy drives accelerated expansion. GR describes gravity as spacetime curvature.
dark-cosmos-gr.example_problem: Dark energy acts like negative pressure; dark matter clusters like matter.

telescopes-types.title: Telescopes and Instruments
telescopes-types.brief_info: Refractors, reflectors, radio dishes, space telescopes above seeing-limited atmosphere.
telescopes-types.example_problem: Large mirrors are easier than huge lenses for astronomy.

angular-resolution.title: Magnification and Angular Resolution
angular-resolution.brief_info: Diffraction limit θ ~ λ/D; bigger aperture or shorter wavelength improves detail; interferometry increases effective baseline.
angular-resolution.example_problem: Large telescopes resolve smaller angles on the sky.

spectroscopy-photometry.title: Spectroscopy and Photometry
spectroscopy-photometry.brief_info: Spectra yield composition, temperature, radial velocity; photometry measures flux through filters.

distance-ladder-radio.title: Cosmic Distances and Radio Astronomy
distance-ladder-radio.brief_info: Parallax, standard candles, then Hubble's law for cosmological distances; radio maps quasars and cold gas.
distance-ladder-radio.example_problem: Very distant galaxies use Hubble relation when individual candles are unavailable.

terrestrial-gas-planets.title: Terrestrial and Gas Giant Planets
terrestrial-gas-planets.brief_info: Mercury to Mars: rocky surfaces. Jupiter and Saturn: mostly H/He. Uranus and Neptune: ice giants.
terrestrial-gas-planets.example_problem: Jupiter has no solid surface like Earth's — layered fluid interior.

moon-satellites.title: The Moon and Major Moons
moon-satellites.brief_info: Phases from Sun–Earth–Moon geometry; tidal locking. Giant-planet moons (Titan, Europa) are astrobiology targets.
moon-satellites.example_problem: We see one lunar face because rotation period equals orbital period.

small-bodies-exoplanets.title: Asteroids, Comets, and Exoplanets
small-bodies-exoplanets.brief_info: Asteroid belt, Kuiper belt, Oort cloud; cometary tails near the Sun; exoplanets via transits and radial velocity.
small-bodies-exoplanets.example_problem: Transits dim a star periodically when a planet crosses its disk.

life-search.title: Habitable Zone and the Search for Life
life-search.brief_info: Liquid-water distance from a star; SETI for technosignatures; missions seek organics and subsurface oceans.
life-search.example_problem: Habitable zone width depends on stellar luminosity.
